coredata.dc:title: Sustainable latent fingerprint enhancement with ink-free printing and shape memory behavior using Parthenium Hysterophorus-derived carbon dots

coredata.dc:description: We developed a method to produce red fluorescent carbon dots from Parthenium hysterophorus leaves (PCDs) through hydrothermal carbonization. These PCDs exhibit consistent photoluminescence, emitting light regardless of excitation, with a 6.01 ns fluorescence lifetime and a 10.2% quantum yield. They possess a fine size of 4.4 nm, excellent hydrophilicity, and stability in a pH range of 4–11. Leveraging their optical properties, these PCDs function as precise nano-probes for detecting Fe3+ ions, demonstrating a linear correlation within 0 to 50 μM concentrations and a low detection limit of approximately 0.14 μM. Emitting light at about 620 nm, these PCDs remain visible even in daylight. When applied to fingerprints on various substrates and dried, they reveal distinct prints under 365 nm UV light, retaining stability for 24 h. This resilience is attributed to preventing fluorescence reduction, achieved through the coffee-ring effect and electrostatic interactions during drying. Employing deep learning with the YoLOv8 algorithm, fluorescence images of these latent fingerprints (LFPs) exceptionally resemble standard controls. These PCDs serve a dual role as fluorescent ink and nanocomposite films within a PVA matrix, demonstrating potential in anti-counterfeiting applications. This study presents a straightforward method for synthesizing PCDs with intense red luminescence, establishing them as a versatile platform for Fe3+ sensing, flexible fluorescent films, latent fingerprints, and anti-counterfeiting pursuits.

head.ce:title: Sustainable latent fingerprint enhancement with ink-free printing and shape memory behavior using Parthenium Hysterophorus-derived carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We developed a method to produce red fluorescent carbon dots from Parthenium hysterophorus leaves (PCDs) through hydrothermal carbonization. These PCDs exhibit consistent photoluminescence, emitting light regardless of excitation, with a 6.01 ns fluorescence lifetime and a 10.2% quantum yield. They possess a fine size of 4.4 nm, excellent hydrophilicity, and stability in a pH range of 4–11. Leveraging their optical properties, these PCDs function as precise nano-probes for detecting Fe3+ ions, demonstrating a linear correlation within 0 to 50 μM concentrations and a low detection limit of approximately 0.14 μM. Emitting light at about 620 nm, these PCDs remain visible even in daylight. When applied to fingerprints on various substrates and dried, they reveal distinct prints under 365 nm UV light, retaining stability for 24 h. This resilience is attributed to preventing fluorescence reduction, achieved through the coffee-ring effect and electrostatic interactions during drying. Employing deep learning with the YoLOv8 algorithm, fluorescence images of these latent fingerprints (LFPs) exceptionally resemble standard controls. These PCDs serve a dual role as fluorescent ink and nanocomposite films within a PVA matrix, demonstrating potential in anti-counterfeiting applications. This study presents a straightforward method for synthesizing PCDs with intense red luminescence, establishing them as a versatile platform for Fe3+ sensing, flexible fluorescent films, latent fingerprints, and anti-counterfeiting pursuits.

Synopsis: This study introduces an eco-friendly method, utilizing Parthenium hysterophorus leaves to produce red fluorescent carbon dots with applications in environmental sensing and anti-counterfeiting technology.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) measuring under the quantum confined size of 10 nm have emerged as a unique subset among carbon nanomaterials (NMs), displaying adjustable fluorescence traits that respond to changes in excitation wavelength. These CDs present numerous advantages compared to traditional fluorescent NMs, boasting exceptional optical properties like extended stability, resistance to photo-bleaching, easy modification, high water solubility, low environmental impact, and favorable compatibility with biological systems [1–3]. Consequently, CDs have gained substantial attention for their roles in bio-imaging, drug delivery, photo-catalysis, light-emitting devices, and fluorescent sensing [4]. Researchers focus heavily on synthesizing CDs due to their extraordinary properties and broad applications. Notably, enhancing CDs is significantly emphasized by introducing hetero-atoms, particularly nitrogen and sulfur. Nitrogen has shown significant promise in elevating CD characteristics, expanding its innovative uses across various fields [4–6].

Laser ablation, arc discharge, hydrothermal carbonization, and electrochemical oxidation techniques have created CDs. Hydrothermal synthesis stands out for its simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and minimal energy consumption [7]. This method enables easy control of overreactions. Nitrogen-containing precursors and natural biomass derivatives have been used in hydrothermal CD synthesis. The rise of green chemistry emphasizes crafting fluorescent CDs sans organic chemicals, favoring biomass derivatives for their abundance, low cost, non-toxicity, and capacity to generate CDs without post-treatment in eco-friendly hydrothermal processes using natural resources. Selection of raw materials and reaction conditions significantly impacts CD synthesis [8,9]. Functional groups on CD surfaces enhance water solubility and optical traits. While the green resource approach proves effective, the ongoing challenge lies in discovering new environmentally friendly resources to fashion CDs with desired properties. Table 1 compares the optical properties, particle size, quantum yield, and applications of synthesized CDs derived from various sources [10–19]. The outcomes reveal that CDs derived from Indian Parthenium hysterophorus leaves exhibit comparable or superior optical properties and applications compared to other natural resources.

Parthenium hysterophorus stands out as a unique and hazardous weed, ranked among the top seven most perilous globally. Flourishing in disturbed environments like riverbanks, roadsides, and wastelands, it encroaches upon agricultural fields, significantly impacting ecology and biodiversity, covering an expanse of two million hectares in India. Literature highlights a 40% loss in crop yield and a 90% decline in forage production attributed to this invasive species. Beyond agricultural concerns, contact with Parthenium poses health hazards to animals, leading to rashes, alopecia, and allergic reactions in addition to afflictions like hay fever, asthma, rhinitis, eczema, and contact dermatitis in humans [20–22]. Effective management remains challenging despite extensive efforts involving mechanical, chemical, biological, and competitive replacement methods. However, exploring the weed's beneficial aspects reveals its leaves composition of various organic compounds, showing promise in removing toxic metals like chromium, cadmium, nickel, and mercury. Additionally, the weed's nanoparticles act as surfactants, stabilizing graphene structures. This discovery unveils intriguing possibilities for utilizing the weed, leveraging its advantageous properties and applications, especially in synthesizing carbon-based nanomaterials, given its substantial carbon content as a photosynthetic plant [23,24].

For more than a century, fingerprint analysis has been integral to criminal investigations, relying on detecting LFPs left behind by eccrine sweat and oily sebum when fingers touch surfaces. These invisible LFPs necessitate visualization enhancement. However, traditional methods suffer from drawbacks like high toxicity, substantial background interference, limited contrast, sensitivity, selectivity, and fuming and powder dusting [25]. Despite being user-friendly, the commonly used powder dusting method poses health risks to examiners due to unavoidable dust, potentially erasing fingerprint details. Chemical reagents used in fuming and chemical processes—like iodine, silver nitrate, cyanoacrylate esters, ninhydrin, and 1,8-diazafluoren-9-one (DFO)—can cause severe harm to skin, eyes, mucous membranes, or DNA. Consequently, various fluorescent materials such as quantum dots (QDs), metal-organic frameworks, up-conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs), gold nanoparticles, carbon dots, and semiconductor polymer dots have been explored for LFP development. These materials offer improved contrast, selectivity, and reduced background interference compared to conventional techniques [26–28].

Over the past decade, fluorescent CDs have gained widespread attention for their cost-effectiveness, environmentally friendly synthesis, stable luminescence, and compatibility with biological systems. Despite their extensive use in bio-imaging, drug delivery, and fluorescence sensing, more reports need to be on their involvement in detecting LFPs [29,30]. Integrating CDs into LFP detection faces several challenges. Firstly, the predominant blue-green emission of most CDs makes them vulnerable to interference from the self-fluorescence of substrates like plastic and paper. Secondly, CDs aggregate in a solid state, hindering effective spraying techniques and leading to fluorescence quenching. Thirdly, reports on CD-based hybrid powders for LFP detection reveal weak fluorescence and insufficient for visible detection without specialized equipment. Additionally, using nanoparticles' powder risks damaging fingerprint details and endangering examiners' health. Finally, the choice of CD dispersion mediums poses challenges; water struggles to adhere to glossy surfaces, while organic solvents can harm LFPs. The search continues for a simple, portable, safe, clear, and compatible method to illuminate LFPs using CDs [31–33].

Counterfeiting continues to emerge as a global menace, impacting manufacturers and consumers profoundly and jeopardizing safety and financial security. These counterfeit goods penetrate supply chains, posing safety risks and causing economic damage. Instances like counterfeit vehicle parts causing accidents or unauthorized replication of pharmaceuticals directly affect human health. Illegally replicated media CDs, branded accessories, ornaments, and electrical components result in significant financial losses. When money, passports, identity cards, and official documents are counterfeited, a nation's economy and security face severe risks. To combat counterfeiting, advanced and highly secure anti-counterfeiting (AC) technologies are imperative [34,35]. Various methods have been employed, such as hashing, digital watermarking, QR/bar codes, micro-printing, color-changing inks, and 3D ribbons. Luminescent material-based AC methods show promise, capitalizing on the distinctive optical properties of luminescent materials. Quantum dots, organic dyes, and organic carbon dots have been explored for AC applications. Yet, their effectiveness is hindered by toxicity, susceptibility to light-induced fading, low stability, and broad emission bands.

In this study, we developed a single-step hydrothermal approach to environmentally friendly PCDs synthesis. Parthenium hysterophorus, selected for its toxicity, high chlorophyll content, and minimal environmental impact, undergoes chemical breakdown at elevated temperatures within a tube furnace. Upon cooling, the resulting structures reassemble, forming carbonaceous nanomaterials. The naturally occurring amino acids in the plant leaves contribute to the material's nitrogen-rich composition, while H2SO4 is an oxidizing and dehydrating agent. Emphasizing sustainability, we maintained a temperature of 180 °C, foregoing activating agents or metal catalysts. Comprehensive physicochemical analysis, including X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, and fluorescence excitation and emission spectroscopy, provided insights into the structural and optical characteristics of the synthesized CDs.

The red-emissive fluorescent PCDs were utilized to visualize LFP using a straightforward spraying technique. The red emission effectively reduced background fluorescence interference, ingeniously addressing the common issue of aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching in PCDs. Our PCDs exhibited the capability to detect LFPs on diverse surfaces, offering promise for practical criminal investigations as a simple, rapid, portable, and precise tool. For fingerprint examination, we developed a deep learning model employing the YOLOv8 algorithm, known for object detection in images or videos. Typically, fingerprint recognition and analysis rely on specialized algorithms identifying unique patterns in fingerprint images, utilizing minutiae points (endings and bifurcations), ridge orientations, and ridge patterns for comparison. This study introduces the first-ever YOLO-based method for fingerprint feature identification, unprecedented in previous reports. Additionally, the PCDs displayed considerable potential as fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting applications. Moreover, by integrating the resulting PCDs into poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) film, we successfully fabricated fluorescent hybrid polymer films (PCDs@PVA).

Parthenium hysterophorus leaves were gathered from roadside vegetation. The following chemicals, each with a purity of 99.0%, were obtained from Sigma Aldrich: concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4), dopamine hydrochloride, phosphate buffer tablet, ethanol, TPH, DMSO, acetonitrile, hexane, toluene, water, 1,2-propanediol, acetonitrile, cyclohexane, diethylene glycol, ethanol, methanol, triethylamine, ascorbic acid, PbCl2, CaCl2, BaCl2, CuCl2, FeCl2, FeCl3, ZnCl2, NiCl2, MgCl2, CoCl2, and CrCl2. Additionally, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA: molecular weight-1500) and deionized (DI) water were utilized to prepare aqueous solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots synthesis from Parthenium hysterophorus
ce:section.ce:para: CD synthesis comprised a two-step procedure. Initially, 50 g of fresh Parthenium hysterophorus leaves underwent collection, washing, maceration, and immersion in an oil bath at 100 °C. The resulting extracted juice was treated with 10 mL of H2SO4 and underwent hydrothermal treatment at 140 °C for 1 h within a muffle furnace. The resulting powder was continuously rinsed with DI water until the pH reached 6. The resulting black powder was treated with ethanol, denoted as PCDs. The utilization of H2SO4 in the process reduced temperature and time requirements, serving as a dehydrating agent owing to the high water content in the biomass leaves. This sustainable approach entailed swift extraction at low temperatures, utilizing readily available and underutilized weed leaves. Scheme 1a provides a schematic illustration of the PCD synthetic procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination method of the concentrations of PCDs in solution
ce:section.ce:para: The following method was employed to prepare a solution with a concentration of 1 μM of PCDs in DMSO solvent. First, we calculated the number of moles using the formula: number of moles equals concentration multiplied by volume (in L). Since the concentration is given in micromolar (μM), it was converted to molar, resulting in 1 μM equals 1 × 10−6 M. Then, the number of moles was calculated to dissolve in 1 L of solvent, resulting in 1 × 10−6 mol. Next, the molar mass of the PCDs, denoted as M-PCDs, was determined. Mass equals the number of moles multiplied by M-PCDs. Given that the molar mass of PCDs is 100 g/mol, the resulting mass was calculated to be 1 × 10−4 g. Therefore, to obtain a concentration of 1 μM, 1 × 10−4 g of PCDs should be dissolved in 1 L of DMSO solvent. Similarly, 5, 10, 20, and 30 μM concentrations can be achieved by dissolving 5, 10, 20, and 30 × 10−4 g of PCDs in 1 L of DMSO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Latent fingerprint detection using the spray method
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized solution, termed PCDs@DMSO (consisting of 5 mg PCDs dissolved in a solution of 10 mL ethanol and 30 mL DMSO), underwent assessment for detecting LFPs on various surfaces. Scheme 1b provides a step-by-step illustration detailing the creation and visualization of LFPs utilizing the spray method. Fingerprints were obtained from a 25-year-old male donor by cleaning hands with soapy water, drying, and pressing the thumb on sebum-covered areas, such as the forehead. The PCDs@DMSO solution was sprayed onto fingerprints present on surfaces like paper, leaves, aluminum foil, glossy covers, metals, and coins. Visualization was conducted under both normal light conditions and 365 nm UV light, captured using a Canon EOS 700D digital camera. Employing the YOLOv8 method, which harnesses deep learning and computer vision advancements, enabled the enhancement and extraction of fingerprint features' minutiae for swift identification. YOLOv8, an object detection algorithm, exhibited remarkable speed and accuracy in fingerprint recognition tasks.

In a pioneering study, Jie Chen et al. [14] leveraged the versatile properties of carbon dots (CDs) to enhance fingerprint detection techniques. Their research focused on synthesizing red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) through a hydrothermal treatment that utilized p-phenylenediamine and phosphorus acid as precursors. This innovative approach sought to capitalize on the unique luminescent properties of CDs for forensic applications. The developed prints were recorded several hours later, utilizing their red fluorescence under ultraviolet (UV) light for optimal visualization. This method showcased a significant improvement in processing time compared to other chemical development techniques, often necessitating lengthy exposure times or complex post-treatment steps. The current study further refined the methodology by employing a solution of PCDs dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). DMSO was selected for its rapid evaporation rate and ability to dissolve various substances, including the PCDs. This solution, PCDs@DMSO, was expediently sprayed onto the latent fingerprints on various substrates such as paper, leaves, aluminum foil, glossy covers, metals, and coins. Remarkably, the development time was reduced to under 30 s, with fingerprint capture under UV light conducted immediately afterward. The advantages of using PCDs@DMSO extend beyond rapid development times. The evaporation of DMSO leaves behind a residue highly concentrated in PCDs, which in turn bind to the lipid constituents of fingerprints. This results in a high-contrast image that can be visualized under UV light without further enhancement or development steps.

A mixture of 5 g PVA with PCDs is utilized to prepare AC ink using PCDs. Achieving a uniform dispersion is crucial. Hence, 0.5 mg of PCDs undergo 1 h of sonication within the PVA medium. The solution then undergoes a 10 min degassing process using a vacuum pumping system to remove air bubbles, resulting in a transparent solution ready for immediate use as security ink. Refer to Scheme 1c for a schematic illustration depicting the creation of AC labels employing a straightforward brush application with AC ink. When these labels are inscribed with luminescent ink, they illuminate under UV light at a 365 nm wavelength, enabling camera image capture.

In an 80 °C oil bath, a solution containing 10 g of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) in 100 mL of deionized (DI) water underwent magnetic stirring until the complete dissolution of PVA. Subsequently, 2–10 mL of a PCDs solution (2 mg/mL) was added and stirred for 1 h, followed by simultaneous sonication for 20 min to ensure uniform dispersion. Following this, the mixture was centrifuged to eliminate any bubbles. The resulting well-dispersed blend was then poured into a petri plate, approximately 10 × 1.5 cm2 in size, at room temperature (RT), resulting PCDs@PVA NCs films (Scheme 1d). For comparison, PVA films without PCDs were created using the same procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield measurement, prolonged and photo-bleaching
ce:section.ce:para:
The reference solution utilized quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4, exhibiting a quantum yield (QY) of approximately ∼54%. Absorbance values (A) across various concentrations were measured using a 350 nm UV light, falling within a range of 0.01 to 0.05. The fluorometer software enabled the determination of integrated fluorescence intensity (area under the spectrum) by scanning the fluorescence spectra of both the reference and sample solutions. The quantum yields (ΦX) of the carbon dots were calculated using the refractive indices of DI water and 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 in the equation below [36]:
(1)
Φ X
=
Φ ST
k X
/
k ST
η X 2
/
η ST 2

The reference material's quantum yield (QY) is denoted as ΦST in the equation, where
η X
and
η ST
represent the refractive indices of the sample and reference materials, respectively. Additionally,
k X
and
k ST
signify the slopes of the linear fitting lines for the sample and reference materials. Long-term (prolonged) and photo-bleaching experiments were conducted on the aqueous solution of PCDs to assess stability. Fluorescence emission spectra were measured at different intervals to compare the fluorescence intensity with the initial PCD solution, which was stored for 180 days to ensure sustained stability. In the photo-bleaching tests, fluorescence emission was recorded at various intervals while exposing the sample to UV radiation (λex = 365 nm). The solution was positioned 100 mm from the UV light source, and the maximum fluorescence intensity was maintained. The fluorescence intensity was monitored every 10 min throughout the 120 min treatment. This comprehensive characterization provides insights into PCD stability under various conditions.

The current study utilized various instruments: (i) A Shimadzu diffractometer (Cu Kα = 1.5406 Å, 45 kV, 200 mA) for collecting powder XRD patterns. (ii) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an Esca lab 250Xi spectrometer. (iii) Microstructure and element composition investigation was carried out using a JEOL-JEM-2100. (iv) A JEM-2010 TEM system for capturing TEM, HR-TEM images, and microscopic chemical composition. (v) Raman spectroscopy employed a micro-Raman spectrometer with a 488 nm excitation wavelength. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using an iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). (vi) UV–VIS spectra were obtained using a Tu-1810 UV–VIS spectrophotometer. (vii) A Horiba spectrofluorometer with a 450 W Xenon lamp recorded excitation and emission spectra for photoluminescence analysis at room temperature. (viii) The FLS920 steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, U.K.) measured fluorescence lifetime.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was employed to examine the crystallinity and graphitization of PCDs. The XRD patterns revealed a prominent peak at around 24.2° (2θ), corresponding to the (002) lattice plane of carbon-based materials with some disordered carbon atoms (Fig. 1a). The introduction of nitrogen and oxygen-containing groups onto the PCD surface contributed to the considerable degree of graphitization observed. The fractional graphitization of PCDs was inferred from their weakest peak at 2θ = 42.7°. Calculations based on the (002) lattice plane indicated an inter-layer spacing of 0.38 nm for the synthesized PCDs, larger than that of bulk graphite (0.34 nm) [37]. This discrepancy is attributed to functional groups, particularly nitrogen and oxygen, predominantly at the edges of PCDs.

Furthermore, Raman spectroscopy provided insights into the degree of graphitization (carbon structure). The Raman spectrum exhibited comparable intensities of the D and G bands (Fig. 1b), indicating a similar amount of defects alongside graphitic carbon. The D band at 1340 cm−1 signifies the presence of sp3 defects and the turbostratic carbon arrangement in the synthesized structure, while the G band at 1568 cm−1 indicates the sp2 carbon order. Additionally, the wider 2D1 and 2G bands at 2620 and 2971 cm−1 represent the structure resembling reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [38]. The ID/IG ratio derived from the Raman spectra was close to one, indicating surface/edge defects of functional groups in the biomass-generated rGO [39]. Various reaction parameters, such as temperature, reaction environment, and starting material, influenced the ID/IG ratio of PCDs. Observations revealed that the ID/IG ratio decreased as temperature increased due to the reduced number of defects within the carbon nanomaterial. For comparison, the ID/IG ratio for graphene oxide is 1.02; for pristine graphene, it is 0.88, consistent with the XRD findings.

FTIR spectroscopy was utilized to examine the functional groups present in PCDs. As depicted in Fig. 1c, the FTIR spectrum displayed peaks at 3421, 2912, 1631, 1573, and 1529 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of NH, CH, CO, CN, CO, and CC, respectively [40]. Additionally, the bending vibration of alkyl was identified at 1466 cm−1. Further stretching vibrations of CN, CF, and CO were observed at 1309, 1269, and 1231 cm−1, respectively [41]. Morphological characteristics captured by TEM exhibited PCDs that were nearly spherical and evenly dispersed, without notable aggregation, as illustrated in the computer-generated image (Fig. 1d). Analyzing particle size distribution is critical in characterizing nanomaterials, as it significantly influences their physical and chemical properties. Our study used ImageJ software to calculate the average size of the nanoparticles within our samples. We employed the normal distribution method. This widely accepted approach in particle size analysis involves fitting a normal (Gaussian) distribution curve to the size measurements obtained from the image data. By plotting the frequency of each measured particle size against its occurrence, we create a distribution curve that describes how the particle sizes are dispersed around the mean value. The result of this analytical approach is a particle size distribution plot, which we have included as Fig. 1e in the revised manuscript. The plot reveals nanoparticles predominantly on the nanometer scale, exhibiting a narrow size distribution. This indicates that our sample is uniform particle size, with minimal deviation from the average. The mean particle size, as depicted by the peak of the distribution curve, is approximately <d ≥4.4 nm.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to analyze individual atoms' chemical composition and oxidation state in PCDs, offering insights into the emitted photoelectron intensity from different atoms. The XPS survey of PCDs (Fig. 1f) unveiled characteristic peaks of C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s at 285, 533, and 401 eV, respectively. These spectra indicated a high carbon content in PCDs, followed by oxygen and nitrogen. Deconvolution of the C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s spectra (Fig. 1g-i) provided a comprehensive analysis of the chemical environments of these three atoms. Within the C 1 s spectra (Fig. 1g), 284, 285, and 286 eV peaks aligned with CC sp2, CC defects/sp3, and C–O/C–N groups, respectively [42,43]. The broad hump in the carbon spectra suggested a blend of graphitic and disordered carbon. Oxygen functionality was evident in Fig. 1h, with prevailing O–C=O and C–O–C groups at 533 and 534 eV. Fig. 1i presented the nitrogen spectrum, showcasing peaks at 398, 399, and 401 eV corresponding to -C-O/C-N, C-NH-C, and NH3 functional groups, respectively, originating from naturally occurring amino acids in Parthenium leaves [44,45]. Table 2 summarizes the percentage of functional groups obtained from the deconvoluted XPS spectra of PCDs.

The remarkable optical properties of the PCDs were investigated through UV–vis absorption and fluorescence (FL) spectra. Two distinct absorption peaks emerged at 277 and 521 nm (Fig. 2a). These are attributed to the π-π* transition of the sp2 aromatic [46] and n-π* transition of surface-attached functional groups [47]. The PCDs displayed their maximum excitation and emission peaks at 540 and 620 nm, resulting in vivid red fluorescence. The normalized intensity figure of the corresponding excitation-dependent emission spectra, as shown in Fig. 2b, exhibits the redshift behavior of the FL maxima. The highest magnitude of FL intensity of PCDs was seen at a wavelength of 620 nm when excited at 540 nm. This suggests that surface structures and states contribute to the red emission, as the visible-range fluorescence excitation spectrum aligns well with the absorption curve. The fluorescence of the CDs is proposed to originate from surface chemical groups, exhibiting excitation-independent photoluminescence (PL) emission. Our PCDs demonstrated robust red fluorescence under UV light. Furthermore, the Commission Internationale de l'Elcairage (CIE) diagram, employed to delve deeper into FL emission, revealed CIE coordinates of (0.618, 0.245), prominently positioned in the red region (Fig. 2c), confirming the exceptional optical prowess of the PCDs.

Metal ion solutions containing PbCl2, CaCl2, CuCl2, BaCl2, ZnCl2, FeCl2, FeCl3, MgCl2, NiCl2, CrCl2, and CoCl2, each at a concentration of 10 μM [48], were utilized to investigate sensing capabilities. Aqueous PCDs combined with these metal solutions were exposed to UV 365 nm light. Fluorescence spectra captured for PCDs mixed with diverse metal solutions at an excitation wavelength of 540 nm (Fig. 2d) depicted reduced emission peak intensities in the presence of Fe3+ ions due to quenching. This quenching could stem from energy transfer between surface groups and the d-orbitals of metal atoms or the formation of a chelating complex involving PCD surface functional groups and Fe3+ ions. Fe3+ ions exhibited markedly strong quenching behavior (Fig. 2e), as indicated by their notably low values in F/Fo. Further exploration of without Fe3+ ion (PCDs only) and with Fe3+ ion detection at varying concentrations (10–40 μM), as shown in Fig. 2f, revealed that PCDs are effectively quenched with Fe3+ ions even at low concentrations. Within the 10–40 μM concentration range, the correlation coefficient (R2) approached 0.932, highlighting a strong relationship, as shown in Fig. 2g. With a limit of detection (LoD) of 2.12 μM, the concentration curve for Fe3+ ions displayed a positive slope, emphasizing robust energy transfer and absorption affinity between PCDs and ferric ions. Due to their sensitivity and selectivity, PCDs could find utility in assessing ferric ion concentrations in polluted water bodies. A comparison of the optical properties of PCDs for Fe3+ detection with other recent studies is detailed in Table 3 [49–56].

PCDs demonstrated favorable solubility in common solvents such as dimethylformamide, tetrahydrofuran, acetone, ethanol, dimethyl sulfoxide, acetonitrile, and water, with notably higher fluorescence intensity observed in organic solvents compared to water. The fluorescence differences among solvents might stem from diverse interactions between solvent molecules and PCDs or the presence of distinct surface trap states in various solvents. Leveraging these unique optical properties and the exceptional solubility of PCDs in organic solvents, we investigated their potential for quantitatively determining trace water in organic solvents using PCDs as fluorescence sensors.

Organic solvent mixtures were initially prepared with varied water concentrations, including Tetrahydrofuran (THF), ethanol, Dimethylformamide (DMF), and acetone. The fluorescence of PCDs in these organic systems was recorded. Taking THF as an example, PCDs powder was dissolved in THF mixed with varying water contents (ranging from 0 to 100%). As shown in Fig. S1a (Supporting Information), the PL of PCDs gradually quenched with increasing water content from 0 to 100%. The fluorescence quenching factor (Fo–F1)/Fo, where Fo and F1 represent the PL intensity of PCDs in the absence and presence of water) exhibited a linear increase in water content from 0 to 10% (Fig. S1b, Supporting Information). Utilizing this linear equation, the correlation coefficient (R2) and the detection limit (LOD = 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of 10 blank measurements, and k is the slope of the function fitting) were determined as 0.991 and 0.244%, respectively.

Similarly, PCDs were employed to detect trace water in ethanol, DMF, and acetone. As demonstrated in Fig. S1c and S1d (Supporting Information) for ethanol, with water content changing from 2 to 12%, the fluorescence quenching factor (Fo–F1)/Fo yielded a detection limit of 0.567% and a correlation coefficient 0.999. In DMF (Fig. S1e, Supporting Information), the PL intensity of PCDs quenched proportionally with water content ranging from 4% to 16%, and the resulting LOD for water in DMF was determined as 0.369% with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.995. Assessing water content in acetone (Fig. S1g, Supporting Information), a linear relationship (R2 = 0.998) between the fluorescence quenching factor and water content (0 to 10%) was observed (Fig. S1h, Supporting Information), with a determined LOD of 0.871%. A summary of the linear relationships, correlation coefficients, linear ranges, and detection limits is presented in Table S1(Supporting Information). Comparing these results with reported CQDs-based fluorescence sensors for water detection, the prepared PCDs exhibited higher sensitivity and lower detection limits (Table S2, Supporting Information).

Figs. 2h exhibit UV–Vis absorption and emission spectra with a 350 nm excitation for Quinine sulfate and PCDs. The absorption peak of PCDs at 320 nm closely corresponds to Quinine sulfate at 348 nm, affirming Quinine sulfate as a suitable reference for quantum yield (QY) calculations. Additionally, Fig. 2i, illustrating the peak emission at 540 nm excitation for both Quinine sulfate and PCDs, reinforces this alignment. To minimize re-absorption effects, maintaining the absorption below 0.10 was ensured. The absorption intensity of each PCD sample was determined, followed by recording the emission spectra of PCDs and Quinine sulfate at an excitation wavelength of 540 nm. Fig. 2
j and k illustrate the correlation between integrated photoluminescence (PL) intensity and absorption intensity, determined through linear regression to calculate the QY of each sample using Eq.
1 , resulting in an obtained QY of 10.2%.

The optical properties of PCDs are further explored through a fluorescence lifetime (FL) study. The FL decay curve of PCDs is impeccably fitted with a tri-exponential function as outlined below [57]:

The photoluminescence (PL) decay function Y(t) evolves and involves three distinctive decay lifetimes: T1, T2, and T3, each associated with fractional contributions represented by A1, A2, and A3. As depicted in Fig. 2l, the decay curve provides detailed insights into various decay regions, as shown in the inset. This exponential behavior highlights three distinct emissive sites on PCDs. T1 represents the direct electron-hole recombination lifetime, whereas T2 and T3 signify extended lifetimes, indicating prolonged exciton relaxation to ground energy levels. The tri-exponential function fit (
R tri 2
= 0.999 >
R bi 2
= 0.998) applied to time-resolved PL decay curves indicates the existence of three distinct emission sites with average lifetimes of (2.483 ± 2.725), (2.484 ± 3.300), and (11.345 ± 1.876) ns. The average PCD lifetime, Tavg = 5.437 ns, aligns with values ranging from a few nanoseconds to tens of nanoseconds.

Fig. 3a displays the evaluation of PCDs@DMSO fluorescent stability over different periods. Impressively, there's no noticeable alteration in their fluorescence intensity from day 1 to day 180 (Fig. 3b). This lack of photo-bleaching, even after 180 days, signifies the robustness of the PCDs structure against environmental conditions. Investigating the behavior of PCDs in various solvents revealed their stable dispersion in ten different solvents after 1 h of sonication, including water, cyclohexane, acetonitrile, ethanol, methanol, diethylene glycol, triethylamine, hexane, toluene, and 1,2-Propanediol, indicating their compatibility with these solvents. Photographs under 365 nm illumination of the PCDs solutions are presented in Fig. 3c, demonstrating their excellent solubility and fluorescence emission (Fig. 3d), albeit with a slight shift in the emission peak likely due to nanoparticle aggregation [58].

The luminescent intensity's dependency on concentration was explored by studying the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of PCDs at varying concentrations (0.1–5 mg/mL). Fig. S2a (Supporting Information) shows that PCDs exhibited concentration-dependent luminescence, reaching peak PL intensity at 4 mg/mL. Furthermore, the fluorescence properties of PCDs in different solvents were examined, revealing the highest fluorescence intensity in THF, followed by C2H5OH, CH3OCH3, CH3CN, DMF, DMSO, and H2O, as illustrated in Fig. S2b (Supporting Information). PCDs were subjected to varying pH levels, ionic strength, temperature fluctuations, and storage duration to assess their fluorescent and chemical stability. Fig. S2c (Supporting Information) showed consistent PL intensity across a pH range of 4 to 10, indicating robust physiological stability. Similarly, the PL intensity remained unaffected by NaCl concentrations ranging from 0.25 to 1.5 mol/L (Fig. S2d), demonstrating stability under high ionic conditions. Moreover, fluorescence spectra of PCDs at different temperatures and after 10 days of storage were recorded. Results displayed negligible variation in PL intensity across temperatures from 20 to 60 °C (Fig. S2e, Supporting Information) and minimal change in fluorescence intensity after 10 days at room temperature (Fig. S2f, Supporting Information). Overall, these findings confirm the excellent colloidal stability of PCDs.

Fig. 4a depicts the quenching and re-quenching behavior of PCDs. Upon adding 15 μM ascorbic acid (AA) to the mixture, the fluorescence of the PCDs is effectively quenched again. Interestingly, the PCD solution transitions to a blue color instead of remaining black, indicating the return of fluorescence. This revival of fluorescence might be due to the release of loosely bound Fe3+ from the PCDs surface facilitated by the strong affinity between AA and Fe3+. Logic gates (LG) performance is evaluated through an ON-OFF experiment using the FL and re-quenching techniques. The FL ON-OFF assay of carbon dots from various sources is outlined in Fig. 4a, emphasizing the electrostatic coordination between AC ions and PCDs. This interaction induces PCDs AA aggregation, forming conjugated amorphous masses that produce aggregation-induced intense FL emission (Fig. 4b). These results highlight the impressive selectivity of PCDs for AA and Fe3+ ions and their dual FL response features in their original synthesized state [59].

Logical gates (LGs) are integral components of logical circuits that receive inputs and, through specific operations, yield corresponding outputs. This study utilizes the PCDs solution's capability to extinguish fluorescence (FL) upon the addition of Fe3+ and to subsequently re-quench FL upon adding AA. The NOT and IMPLICATION LGs are employed here to assess the quenching and re-quenching behavior of PCDs under diverse input scenarios. The NOT gate functions as an inverter, reversing its input and output. In this context, the presence of Fe3+ corresponds to input 1, leading to FL quenching in PCDs, while its absence corresponds to input 0. A truth table and LG diagram are formulated based on this criterion. The IMPLY gate establishes a logical relationship between two statements. For the implication, LG, Fe3+, and AA are input states “1,” whereas their absence represents “0”. The resulting outputs for each input condition showcase the potential of the synthesized PCDs in advancing sophisticated molecular logic devices.

Fig. 4c showcases the NOT LG and its corresponding truth table. For the NOT gate to function effectively, the fluorescence quenching of PCDs is crucial, where input 1 represents the presence of Fe3+ and input 0 signifies its absence. The PCDs solution exhibits maximum intensity when Fe3+ is absent, indicating an output of 1 (ON state). Conversely, when Fe3+ is present, no intensity is observed, indicating an output of 0 (OFF state). This data is used to construct an accurate truth table and LG diagrams. The IMPLY gate in digital logic connects two propositions logically; one input undergoes a NOT gate while the other utilizes an OR gate. Leveraging this fundamental concept, LG's implications are built and assessed. The inverted output from the NOT gate feeds into the second input of the OR gate, with the final output of the IMPLY gate presumed to match the OR gate's output. The functionality of the IMPLICATION LG and its corresponding truth table relies on AA-sensitive switching technology and Fe3+, as depicted in Fig. 4d. The “1” state occurs with the presence of Fe3+ and AA, while the “0” state is when these elements are absent. For each input condition, the output is evaluated. When neither Fe3+ nor AA (0/0 state) is present, the PCDs solution exhibits maximum FL, resulting in an output 1 (ON state). Conversely, when AA is absent, and Fe3+ (0/1) is the input condition, no FL is observed, leading to an output of 0 (OFF state). With AA present but Fe3+ absent (1/0), the PCDs solution fluoresces, producing an output of 1 (ON state). Finally, AA and Fe3+ present (1/1) generate FL, resulting in an output 1 (ON state). These findings underscore the potential of synthesized PCDs in the development of the next generation of molecular logic circuits [60].

Fluorescence imaging is integral to LFP detection and is prized for its high contrast and minimal background interference. Recent strides in CDs show promise for LFP advancement, especially through the favored solution method, known for its simplicity and reduced dye usage. Leveraging synthesized PCDs with robust red emission and low hazardous properties, a spray method is employed for LFP detection [14]. PCDs solutions undergo validation for sensitivity and selectivity across diverse surfaces. Fig. 5a presents a visual sensitivity comparison among PCDs, PCDs@acetonitrile, PCDs@Water, PCDs@TPH, and PCDs@DMSO. The PCDs@DMSO solution yields a more precise, high-contrast image under 365 nm UV exposure than other solutions. To gauge the effect on LFP development, fingerprints on paper receive different concentrations of the PCDs@DMSO solution. The fingerprint becomes discernible gradually, with clear images emerging at 1, 5, 10, and 20 μM concentrations, significantly enhancing at 30 μM, as shown in Fig. 5b.

On a metal plate, the letters “CNR” were inscribed using solutions composed of the primary constituents of LFPs, aiming to identify the specific components that could interact with PCDs. This mixture encompassed glycine, glucose, urea, lysozyme, sodium chloride, lactic acid, cholesterol, and lipids (a blend of oleic acid and lauric acid triglyceride). After drying, the characters were immersed in an aqueous PCDs@DMSO solution (30 μM). Only the “CNR” pattern, drawn with the lipid's component, exhibited strong fluorescence and became visible under 365 nm UV light. This outcome demonstrated that the PCDs specifically stained the lipid molecules in the LFPs, as depicted in Fig. 5c. When PCDs come into contact with LFPs, the PCDs molecules seem to adhere to lipid secretions, likely due to interactions between their lipophilic end and hydrophobic-hydrophobic groups [61]. Additionally, the adhesion effect might be influenced by electrostatic interactions between the naturally negative charge of lipid secretions, such as fatty acids, and the positive charge of PCDs [61,62]. While PCDs usually did not emit light in an aqueous solution due to non-radiative transitions, the strong fluorescence observed when PCDs are paired with lipid secretions in LFPs is likely due to the limitation of intra-molecular motion caused by the viscosity effect of lipid secretions, as shown in Fig. 5d.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of LFP identification via spray method
ce:section.ce:para: Using the optimal PCDs@DMSO solution (30 μM), fingerprints (FPs) were effectively identified on various porous, semi-porous, and non-porous surfaces. In Fig. 6a, detailed red fluorescence images of FPs were captured on paper, leaves, aluminum foil, glossy cover, metal, and coins under 365 nm light without additional treatment. The clear visibility of fingerprint structures in the fluorescence images showcases the enhanced selectivity and specific adherence of the PCDs@DMSO solution. This solution uniformly adheres to the FPs, particularly attaching to the ridges rather than the furrows, resulting in highly illuminated red ridges. This robust interaction with the ridges significantly enhances FP image development. The strength of the red fluorescence effectively resists interference from ambient light, including UV light reflection on nonporous surfaces or glossy covers. The structural characteristics of fingerprint ridges are categorized into three levels: level I describes the pattern type, while levels II and III pertain to individual-specific details [63,64,65]. The developed FPs exhibit a consistent and distinct alignment of nanoparticles (NPs) along the ridges, as evident in the three-dimensional (3D) surface imaging (Fig. 6b). This alignment suggests strong binding with fingerprint residues. Analyzing pixel value fluctuations in the selected region (white strip) in Fig. 6c delineates grooves and ridges with attached PCDs, highlighting the high-resolution capability of LFP detection. Overall, PCDs@DMSO demonstrates promising applications in LFP detection. In Fig. 7a, LFPs on a paper substrate are displayed, developed through the spray method, and observed under UV 365 nm light. These images exhibit various ridge patterns, flows, and directions. The magnified images reveal detailed structures at different levels, including core and delta in level I; island, eye, hook, dot, bifurcation, double bifurcation, short ridge, bridge, and enclosure in level II; and incipient ridges, scar, and sweat pores in level III. These details are pivotal for individual fingerprint-based identification, emphasizing the significance of sweat pores in identifying individuals, even with imperfect or fragmented fingerprints [66–68].

Poroscopic parameters, encompassing sweat pore positions, numbers, inter-spacing, area, shapes, and angles, were meticulously analyzed on the developed fingerprints (FPs) utilizing ImageJ [69] and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) [70], as depicted in Fig. 7b-i. The tabulated data in Table 4 encapsulates estimations of sweat pore numbers per unit area, their positions, inter-spacing, shapes, ridge width, and furrow width. Employing ImageJ software and SEM image, the average count of pores per friction ridge unit length was determined, resulting in an average of 7 pores per unit length of friction ridge. This finding is consistent with observations by Locard, O'Leary [71], and Bindra et al. [72], who reported varying pore numbers per centimeter of friction ridge. Bindra et al. specifically noted 8 to 25 pores/cm of the epidermal ridge. Pore deformations, influenced by skin elasticity and compression, lead to shape, size, and type changes.

Fig. 7
b and f illustrate the pore counts per unit length of the FPs using ImageJ and SEM. The comprehensive analysis of these poroscopic parameters contributes to understanding individual fingerprint characteristics and underscores the potential for precise and unique fingerprint identification. The consistent inter-pore distances, regardless of pressure distortions or deviations in ridge paths, establish a reproducible pattern unique to each individual. The interconnected sweat pore centers within the same ridge and between neighboring ridges yield distinct and individualized patterns. Fig. 7
b and f vividly illustrate the inter-spacing of sweat pores in the developed fingerprints (FPs) using ImageJ and SEM. Studies by Oklevski et al. [73] spanning a 5–48-year period examining pores in 100 pairs of fingerprints highlighted the stability of the spatial arrangement of sweat pores even over a 48-year interval. Similarly, Monson et al. [74] observed persistent pore locations after 29 years, emphasizing the long-term stability of pore spatial arrangement. Anthonioz et al. [75], utilizing high-resolution images and co-axial illumination, investigated pore configurations relative to ridge characteristics to distinguish between same-source and different-source fingerprints.

Although crime scenes often yield high-quality images, this study aims to elucidate the significance of sweat pores with the utmost clarity. This detailed investigation assists fingerprint (FP) specialists in evaluating the strength of evidence associated with pores. Observations indicate that combining pores with ridge features enhances discrimination capabilities. Principal component analysis (PCA) was used by Parsons et al. [76] to extract potential pores on a grayscale basis and determine if the pore patterns originated from the same or different fingers. A superimposition-based pore-pattern-matching tool was developed using ImageJ and MATLAB software, confirming the accuracy of pore inter-spacing by comparing an image with red microdots to a LFPs image of the same finger created using ninhydrin. Similarly, Wooá Lee and Juná Park [77] mapped sweat pores using a water-responsive fluorescing PVP composite film, providing colorimetric separation of sweat-secreting active and inactive pores from a digitally scanned FP image while demonstrating reliable superimposition of fluorescence microdots.

Sweat pores manifest at the center or periphery of the papillary ridge, varying in size and type, with closed pores at the center and open pores on one or both sides towards the periphery. PCD adhesion on the pore edge determines the type of pore detected. Print development techniques, PCD consistency, deposition angle, and pressure during fingerprinting can influence this. Fig. 7
b and f show the distribution of pores on ridge surfaces on glass, revealing more closed pores than open ones on either end. Sharma et al. research [78] highlights correlations between pore numbers and central and peripheral positions (per unit length and area) for the thumb, ring, index, and little fingers.

Fig. 7
d and h depict the diameters of created fingerprint pores on glass. Male donor pore diameters average 89–103 μm, while female donor pores range from 76 to 82 μm. The findings demonstrate varied pore sizes coexisting on a single ridge, with large pores being rare, medium-sized ones most common, and small-sized ones following closely. While modern studies report pore sizes between 69 and 284 μm for males and 66–287 μm for females, historical data by Locard noted 88–220 μm diameters in male fingerprints. Considering sweat pore physiological activity, measurements via hydrochromic sensors and a fluorescein-polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) composite film suggest pore diameters can vary based on secretory activity. This raises concerns regarding the accuracy of these measurements in fingerprint matching.

Pore shapes vary widely, spanning square, rhomboid, triangular, oval, elliptical, and circular forms. Pressure distortions can disrupt the original shape of pores, making their configuration inconsistent. Fig. 7
c and g display the pore shapes in developed fingerprints analyzed via ImageJ and SEM. Although Bindra et al. identified rhomboid shapes as the most frequent, followed by circular, elliptical, and rectangular pores, recent studies suggest commonality among circular, oval, and round shapes. In this examination, circular, oval, square, and triangular pores were observed. Fig. 7
c and g also outline the angles between adjacent pores in a developed male fingerprint, measuring between 172 and 177°, consistent with prior research. The results, detailed in Table 4 and shown in Fig. 7
e and i, illustrate the effective adherence of NPs to the friction ridges. Under UV light exposure, NPs emit red along the ridges and appear black in the grooves. Table 4 delineates the most authenticated level-III fingerprint attributes, encompassing all ridge dimensional properties. The substantial variability in level-III dimensionality among donors supports the assertion that no two individuals have identical fingerprints [79].

Pixel profiles of selected regions ( Fig. 7
j and k) and fluctuations in pixel values of the ridge (red) and furrow (black) regions highlight distinct differences. Ridges exhibit high pixel values, while furrows display lower values. The pixel profile of a smaller fingerprint region (Figures 7j
1 and 7k
1 ) emphasizes the distinction between ridges (red, high pixel value) and sweat pores (black, low pixel value), demonstrating their easy differentiation. Notably, the PCDs@DMSO (30 ml) solution selectively adheres to ridges rather than sweat pores. In essence, these findings furnish compelling evidence for individual identification, affirming the utility of the PCDs@DMSO (30 ml) solution for LFPs identification.

Detecting aged fingerprints poses a considerable challenge in forensic investigations due to potential dust accumulation and the natural degradation of fingerprint detail over time. This study scrutinized aged fingerprints over 180 days at 30-day intervals. As depicted in Fig. S3a (Supporting Information), digital images of LFPs from 1 day to 180 days under 365 nm UV radiation illustrate the sustained distinguishability of the ridge patterns, even after the extended period. Fig. S3b (Supporting Information) illustrates distinct LFPs on paper developed with PCDs@DMSO (30 μM) under a high-intensity UV source for up to 60 min, confirming the consistency and dependability of the solution for effective LFP detection. The pixel profile (Fig. S3c, Supporting Information) vividly demonstrates the contrasting intensities between ridges and furrows. Human finger ridges, marked by sweat pores near eccrine glands, leave a distinct impression composed mostly of water (>99%) along with minerals (∼0.5%) and organic substances (∼0.5%) like sugars, amino acids, lactic acids, uric acids, chlorine, urea, and other chemicals. Over time, water slowly evaporates from the ridge surface, leaving behind identifiable oily residues, making ancient ridge patterns harder to trace. However, this study establishes the clear observation of finger ridges even after 180 days under normal environmental conditions. Systematic experiments were repeated to verify the reproducibility and reliability of PCDs@DMSO for LFP detection. In Fig. S3d (Supporting Information), fluctuations in red values in the selected region showcase the high red value in ridge regions and the low red value in furrow regions. These findings underscore the promising potential of these products for LFPs detection. Additionally, aging experiments were conducted using water as a solvent, compared with PCDs@DMSO, as shown in Fig. S4 (Supporting Information). The fingerprints developed with PCDs@Water retained level I & II ridges for up to 30 days before completely vanishing. These results suggest that PCDs@DMSO is highly effective for developing latent fingerprints.

In Figs. 8a-d, aged fingerprints (7, 15, and 60 days) on a glass substrate were re-developed using a PCDs@DMSO (30 μM) solution via spray. These images demonstrate that even after 60 days of aging, the re-developed fingerprints display more precise and high-contrast images under 365 nm UV illumination. Fig. 8
e and f exhibit pixel profiles of selected areas of the aged and re-developed fingerprints. The fluctuation in red values showcases the distinction between the fingerprint's ridge (red) and furrow (black) regions. The experiment's results illustrate LFPs on the glass substrate observed with red phosphorescence, effectively distinguishing between the ridges and furrows of the fingerprint. Comparing the re-developed fingerprints with the aged ones, the red value is notably higher in the re-developed ones. This suggests that the PCDs@DMSO (30 μM) solution significantly enhances the visualization effect of LFPs, displaying high sensitivity in visualizing them.

Figure S5a (Supporting Information) displays non-developed fingerprints on various substrates like paper, glass, and aluminum foil, visible only under specific illumination angles but lacking detailed differentiation [80]. These LFPs require staining methods for practical application. Transitioning to Fig. S5b (Supporting Information), substrates treated with PCDs@DMSO (30 μM) solution without fingerprints are presented. Fig. S5c (Supporting Information) illustrates the chronological process after applying PCDs@DMSO (30 μM) solution on aluminum foil without fingerprints. During the drying phase under UV light, the red fluorescent spots initially decrease and eventually disappear from the aluminum foil. This outcome confirms that, in the absence of fingerprint residues, the PCDs@DMSO (30 μM) solution quickly evaporates under UV light, losing its fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FPs analysis through the deep learning method
ce:section.ce:para: The YOLOv8 (You Only Look Once version 8) deep learning method has gained traction as a real-time object detector due to its accuracy, speed (over 60 frames per second), and efficiency on minimal hardware compared to other deep CNN detectors. To enhance FP feature detection, we curated a specialized dataset encompassing various fingerprint types developed by PCDs, PCDs@acetonitrile, PCDs@TPH, and PCDs@DMSO, annotating it with bounding boxes for minutiae objects like ridge ends and bifurcations. Through a training process involving numerous fingerprint images and associated manual annotations, the YOLOv8 network learned to identify fingerprint minutiae patterns and features.

The training process involved using sigmoid activation and the rectified linear unit (ReLU) functions over 39 epochs to refine the models. Implementation occurred on a V100 server with an Intel(R) Core(TM) i5–3470 CPU and 32 GB RAM. Once trained, the model efficiently detects and localizes minute fingerprint characteristics in unseen images. The algorithm extracts features from input fingerprint images by employing convolutional and pooling layers, utilizing bounding boxes to predict the presence and positions of minutiae points [81]. The algorithm's output comprises bounding boxes encapsulating the detected minutiae points, offering spatial minutiae location data for subsequent processing and analysis, such as matching and verification against known fingerprint databases.

In the present work, our algorithm for fingerprint recognition offers a rapid and precise means to identify and localize minute details within fingerprint images, enabling automated fingerprint recognition systems for diverse applications, including biometric identification, access control, and law enforcement [82]. Certainly, the computational analysis process for fingerprint identification outlined in Fig. 9 involves several key steps when utilizing the object detection methods:

1. Pre-processing: As shown in Fig. 9a, original input fingerprint images undergo pre-processing before being used in deep learning for feature extraction. This step is crucial to maintain experimental accuracy. Pre-processing includes tasks like resizing, normalization, annotation, binarization, and other transformations to ensure compatibility with the model [83]. The objective function assesses the deep algorithm's performance by reducing errors through loss function evaluation.

2. Training Model: The training model phase involves utilizing four pre-processed fingerprint images with pre-trained weights for training on the model (Fig. 9b). Although these fingerprint images share similar characteristics like image size ratio and object size, they differ in illumination due to their creation using various solutions: PCDs, PCDs@acetonitrile, PCDs@TPH, and PCDs@DMSO. Based on convolutional neural networks (CNNs), the model recognizes features within fingerprint images [84]. This computational analysis process, consisting of pre-processing and training using the model, enables accurate and detailed feature recognition within fingerprint images despite variations in illumination and solutions used in their creation.

3. Prediction: Absolutely, the prediction phase in YOLOv8 involves refining bounding box coordinates and filtering out low-confidence detections through post-processing steps, as shown in Fig. 9c, typically outlined as follows: a. Bounding Box Refinement: The initial predictions include bounding boxes, class probabilities, and confidence scores [81,82,85]. These are refined and adjusted to fit the detected objects better. b. Handling Overlapping Boxes: Overlapping bounding boxes can cause redundancy. To address this, non-maximum suppression (NMS) is applied. It selects the most confident and reliable detections while removing redundant or overlapping boxes. c. Final Output: The output from the YOLOv8 analysis is a list of detected fingerprint features, including associated bounding boxes and class labels. These refined predictions and data can be further processed, visualized, or used for subsequent tasks like object tracking, recognition, or classification. By refining the bounding boxes and selecting the most confident detections, these post-processing steps ensure that the final output is optimized for accuracy and utility in downstream applications. In the accompanying Supporting Information (Note S1), we detailed the future enhancements in feature detection for structure modification, image formatting, evaluation metrics, and Intersection over Union (IoU) used in this study.

Figs. 10a-d display fingerprints developed using various solutions like PCDs, PCDs@acetonitrile, PCDs@TPH, and PCDs@DMSO on different surfaces, captured under 365 nm UV light. As previously mentioned, the PCDs@DMSO solution notably provides a more precise and high-contrast image under this UV exposure than other solutions. The direct use of fingerprint images in deep learning for dimensionality reduction and feature extraction significantly impacts experimental accuracy. While the current fingerprint preparation technique is nearly ideal, we propose an innovative YOLOv8x embedded deep learning model that swiftly recognizes features. This model treats the entire fingerprint as a single entity to predict bounding box coordinates. Using a deep convolutional neural network (DCNN), our YOLOv8x identifies bifurcation and ridge end features in Figs. 10e-p, trained over 39 epochs for bifurcation and 49 epochs for ridge end using labeled input fingerprints. Each area in the model operates on a 7 × 7 grid capable of predicting five boundary boxes. Utilizing binarization in pre-processing (Figs. 10i-l) and conditional probability-based bounding box determination with a confidence score of 0.3 through logistic regression, the proposed YOLOv8 model exhibits remarkable speed and efficiency. Figs. 10m-p illustrate True Positive FP images where the model confidently identifies faults at a score of 0.3, showcasing its accuracy.

The outcomes presented in Figs. S6a-f and S7a-f (Supporting Information) across different epochs facilitate a comparison between the performance of the modified method and the baseline approach (the original YOLOv8x). Enhanced precision and recall curves, tending closer to one, typically denote improved fingerprint identification techniques. The precision, recall, and mAP curves for our YOLOv8 model suggest values hovering around 0.7 initially, indicating potential enhancements. The discernible improvement in detection performance using the enhanced method is evident. Figs. S6e-f and S7e-f (Supporting Information) display the box loss (box_loss), classification loss (cls_loss), and distribution focal loss (dfl_loss) of the improved FP-YOLO base model after 39 and 49 training rounds, respectively, along with the YOLOv8x models detection results on the validation set. Moreover, the improved model exhibits more comprehensive features, reduced missed and false detection rates, and higher confidence in its outcomes. The model's responsiveness at various confidence levels is detailed in Figs. S8a, S8c-d, S9a, and S9c-d (Supporting Information), comprising precision, F1, and recall curves, with a confidence level of 1 as a benchmark for optimal model performance. Furthermore, Fig. S8b and Fig. S9b (Supporting Information) depict the PR (precision-recall) curve for the ridge end and bifurcation dataset using the YOLOv8x model alongside the Precision, Recall, and F1 curves.

In summary, the visualization of fingerprints illuminated under 365 nm UV light underscores the significance of deep learning for enhancing experimental accuracy through dimensionality reduction and feature extraction. The YOLOv8 model exhibits notable accuracy in fingerprint visualization, particularly with the PCDs@DMSO solution, offering superior precision and contrast under 365 nm UV light compared to other methods. Despite the effectiveness of current fingerprint preparation techniques, there's a demand for a ground-breaking real-time feature identification strategy. Our proposed YOLOv8x embedded deep learning model addresses this need by precisely predicting bounding box coordinates and class probabilities at high processing speeds. Additionally, our deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) achieves high accuracy in predicting bifurcation and ridge end features, complemented by rigorous training and pre-processing methods like binarization, enabling robust fault detection and efficient performance. The precision and training box dependency outcomes validate our model's capability in identifying bifurcation and ridge end features within fingerprints. Overall, this study introduces a resilient and effective approach to fingerprint analysis, paving the way for enhanced identification and advancements in forensic science applications [86,87].

The PCDs solution displayed remarkable fluorescence, stability, and dispersibility. Fig. 11a exhibits optical images of this solution, revealing a transition from red to a vibrant blue hue under 365 nm UV light. Leveraging its brush-friendly nature, the solution was used to effortlessly script AC labels on diverse surfaces without prior treatment, showcasing vivid red fluorescence under UV light across glass, leaves, debit cards, and more. This eco-friendly, washable, and biocompatible ink shows potential as a replacement for traditional fluorescent inks, particularly in anti-counterfeiting measures. Hand-drawn images maintain their intense fluorescence for up to 120 days, showcasing the impressive stability of the PCDs fluorescence. The resulting transparent and easy-to-clean ink holds promise for fluorescent sketch pens, remaining invisible in daylight for potential encryption applications. Fig. 11b illustrates the vivid red fluorescence of the hand-drawn images under UV light, emphasizing the ink's potential for encryption purposes. The photo-stability of PCDs is evident in Fig. 11c, displaying consistent fluorescence even after prolonged UV exposure, highlighting their enduring characteristics [88–91].

Fig. 12a visually showcases the PCDs@PVA film at varying PCDs concentrations (2–10 mL) under both daylight and UV 365 nm light, demonstrating an incremental increase in fluorescence as the PCDs content rises. This highlights the potential of these engineered PCDs for applications in photovoltaic materials. Shifting to chromaticity, Fig. 12b illustrates the CIE coordinates of PVA and PCDs@PVA film under different lighting conditions. The coordinates of PVA shift from (0.311, 0.304) to (0.253, 0.266) between daylight and UV light, while those of PCDs@PVA demonstrate a distinct shift from (0.329, 0.346) to (0.618, 0.245) [92]. These measurements, conducted using color mixing and photometry software, underscore the influence of PCDs on the film's color characteristics. Furthermore, Fig. 12c depicts the solid-state PL emission peak of PCDs@PVA, excited at 540 nm and centered at 620 nm, showcasing its red fluorescence properties.

Consistency in the maximum emission wavelength across the 480 nm to 560 nm excitation range (Fig. 12d) showcases the excitation-independent nature of PCDs@PVA, corroborating the findings from the PCDs fluorescence spectrum. Leveraging these exceptional optoelectronic properties, the PCDs@PVA film emerges as a promising candidate for solid-state solar cells, marking a significant advancement in PCDs' utilization within photovoltaic materials. The optical transparency of the resulting AC film is evident in Fig. 12e-g, boasting an impressive 87% transmittance and minimal 6% optical haze at 540 nm. In response to the rise in counterfeit products, PCDs seamlessly integrate into a PVA medium, yielding a transparent AC film as depicted in Fig. 12h. These films display remarkable flexibility across various folding scenarios (1 fold, 2 folds, multi-folds, twisting, and rolling), highlighting their suitability for various applications in flexible electronics [93]. The striking red emission under UV 365 nm light further underscores that PVA upholds the exceptional performance of PCDs in photo-luminescence assessments. Assessing the mechanical stability of our PCDs@PVA flexible films, we subjected them to mechanical loads of 0–5 tons for 20 min each (Fig. 12i). Upon releasing the mechanical stress, the films exhibited consistent behavior under normal and UV 365 nm light, indicating their high mechanical resilience and suitability for applications in traffic signaling and solid-state lighting.

Furthermore, photoluminescence investigations assessed the PL intensity distribution in orange-red emitting flexible films under different mechanical strains (0–5 tons), as illustrated in Fig. 12j. Employing a 320 nm excitation wavelength for PL mapping within the black-dotted circle revealed a homogeneous PL intensity distribution in the films, suggesting their potential use in flexible display devices. Flexibility remains a crucial attribute determining the film's applicability in AC applications.

The investigation into the shape memory and fluorescence capabilities of PCDs@PVA NCs films utilized the molecular switch properties of hydrogen bonds. Thin strips of the NCs underwent a bending test to assess shape recovery under various stimuli: water, pH changes, and heat [94]. Initially, the strips were deformed by exposure to an 80 °C oven for 5 min, fixed in a temporary shape at RT, and then subjected to different pH solutions to evaluate shape recovery. The process, detailed in Fig. S10a (Supporting Information), demonstrates the gradual return of the distorted PCDs@PVA strips to their original shape within approximately 120 s, showcasing the NCs shape memory behavior influenced by environmental stimuli. While the neutral aqueous solution gradually weakened fluorescence in the PCDs@PVA NCs sample over time, fluorescence persisted to some extent. Figs. S10b and S10c (Supporting Information) illustrate the impact of alkaline and acidic environments on deformed PCDs@PVA strips. In an alkaline environment, the strips achieved a record shape recovery of 146° in 120 s, accompanied by increasing fluorescence intensity. Conversely, exposure to an acidic environment resulted in a recovery angle of 150° in 90 s but with a gradual decrease in fluorescence, which disappeared upon returning to the original shape. Additionally, rapid immersion in a 70 °C heat atmosphere enabled the deformed strips to regain an angle of 153° within 10 s, demonstrating their responsiveness to temperature changes (Fig. S10d, Supporting Information).

This pH-responsive fluorescence arises from the different ionization states of the carboxyl groups on the PCDs surface in acidic and alkaline solutions. In acidic environments, protonation occurs, leading to electron absorption by the carboxyl groups, creating surface defects and lowering fluorescence intensity. Conversely, in alkaline environments, deprotonation results in increased negative charge, reducing electron absorption, enhancing conjugation, and decreasing surface defects, leading to more intense fluorescence. The formation and dissolution of hydrogen bonds between PCDs and PVA, influenced by the protonation and deprotonation of carboxyl groups, affect both the shape recovery rate and fluorescence intensity. Particularly, alkaline conditions exhibit slower shape recovery rates compared to acidic conditions. The ability of PCDs@PVA NCs to modulate shape recovery rates and fluorescence intensity through pH and temperature manipulation highlights their potential applications in diverse conditions.

The exploration into fingerprint visualization and printing applications led to using PCDs@PVA NCs as substrates for ink-free patterns. An example involved printing the lord GANESHA character, as depicted in Fig. 13a. Coating a metal relief template with the PCDs@PVA NCs solution, forming a transparent film post-solvent evaporation. Upon peeling this film off the template, fluorescent words emerged distinctly, particularly at the pattern's edge region, displaying heightened fluorescence ( Fig. 13
b and c). This visual effect occurred as the PCDs gravitated towards the relief area during solvent evaporation. Entropy within the PCDs/polymer composite system further facilitated the migration of PCDs to the interface between the film and the substrate. Notably, the PCDs@PVA films generated on relief templates encouraged the separation of PCDs towards the interface with the patterned/embossed part (Fig. 13d). Leveraging this visualization mechanism, a GANESHA pattern was successfully created on PCDs@PVA films ( Fig. 13
e and f). This ink-free printing method extended its efficacy to transferring and visualizing relief patterns on stone seals.

We delved into the fluorescence alterations in PCDs@PVA films under diverse environmental conditions, exploring their resilience post-ink-free printing and leveraging the multi-stimulus responsive shape memory and fluorescence traits of PCDs@PVA NCs [91]. The luminous patterns crafted by PCDs@PVA films displayed robustness against heat, external pressure, and alkaline solutions. Specifically, “GANESHA” designs transferred from stone seals were subjected to folding, distortion, and heating up to approximately 100 °C. Upon the films regaining their structure under UV light, the fluorescence intensity of the surface patterns remained unchanged (Fig. 13g). In parallel, when the PCDs@PVA film, obtained from the stone seal and subjected to heat-induced folding and deformation, was immersed in a robust alkaline solution of pH ∼12, the surface pattern's fluorescence intensity escalated during the shape recovery process under UV light (Fig. 13h). These findings align with observed fluorescence variations in the PCDs@PVA NCs film strip during the heat and alkali solution-induced shape recovery processes. The multifaceted capabilities of PCDs@PVA NCs open new avenues for sophisticated anti-counterfeiting and information encryption technologies, especially in demanding pH and high-temperature scenarios.

In summary, we have synthesized red-emitting CDs from Parthenium hysterophorus leaves via a hydrothermal method. These PCDs are spherical, averaging ∼4.4 nm in diameter. Surface-functionalized with oxygen-containing groups, they boast enhanced water solubility and fluorescence. These PCDs exhibit selective FL quenching, a reasonable FL quantum yield of 10.2%, and serve as sensitive probes for Fe3+ ion detection. The ink created from these PCDs enables encryption and decryption in letter writing. With a low limit of detection (LoD) of 0.14 μM and a concentration range of 0–50 μM, the Fe3+ ion detection shows a strong linear relationship (R2 = 0.932). The spray method enables LFPs visualization on diverse substrates, revealing intricate level III details such as ridge width and sweat pores. Even with incomplete LFPs, detailed analysis allows for reliable identity determination. Our YOLOv8x embedded model accurately predicts bounding box coordinates and class probabilities at remarkable processing speeds. Our deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) achieves high accuracy in estimating ridge end and bifurcation features, efficiently detecting faults through comprehensive training and pre-processing techniques. This study highlights PCDs potential for LFPs development and the creation of fluorescent ink for AC applications. The successful integration of PCDs into polymer films offers diverse possibilities, including LFP identification, AC measures, Fe3+ sensing, and flexible display applications.
